content: Mark walked into the interview room expecting the usual stiffness—a handshake, a few polite questions, then a polite goodbye. Instead, he found himself face-to-face with a panel of four people staring silently, their eyes not blinking. One slid a mirror across the table. “Look inside,” they said. Confused, Mark peered at his reflection, then at them. “Why?” he asked. No answer. Then, a voice came from nowhere: “Prove you’re not a liar.” Suddenly, the room filled with flashing lights and questions about his deepest fears, failures, and dreams. A projector displayed his social media, texts, even private emails. Mark tried to protest, but they just nodded, taking notes on his every hesitation. After what felt like hours, the panel vanished. The door locked behind him. He left, jobless, feeling exposed, wondering if this was real or some cruel joke. The worst interview? Not because of the questions, but because they stripped away everything he thought was private.
